title: Lesson 407
transcript: RAID, redundant array of independent disks. RAID is all about using multiple hard drives in a system to achieve one or both of two different things, greater speed or greater redundancy or both. So that's fundamentally what RAID is all about. Redundant array of independent disks is all about using, instead of a single hard drive, let's use multiple hard drives in a system. And by using multiple hard drives, we can achieve either greater speed, greater redundancy, or both. Now, there's all sorts of different types of RAID, different flavors of RAID out there, but there's fundamentally only three that you need to know about for the exam. RAID 0, RAID 1, and RAID 5. And in just a moment here, I'll show you how they work, but let's just start with some naming, because you need to remember this. So RAID 0 is often referred to as striping. And I'll show you what striping is here in just a second. RAID 1 is known as mirroring. And then RAID 5 uses magical parity protection data. I'll show you what this magical parity protection data is soon. So just for now, remember these names. RAID 0, striping. RAID 1, mirroring. And RAID 5, parity protection. OK, let's start with RAID 0. And let me explain this diagram here, what this is trying to show you. First thing here is we have what's called the RAID controller. This is typically hardware. It can be software, but for much better performance, you want this to be an actual hardware device. And actually, I have one right here. Here's an actual RAID controller. This plugs into a PCI slot, and then there's four SATA connections on the back here, so you can connect four hard drives to it. This is a really cheap RAID controller, but it gives you an idea of what they look like. This is what a hardware RAID controller would look like. This one just died in one of my servers recently, so I had it sitting here. OK, now here's why you need a RAID controller. Because like I said, the whole point of RAID is to split data, to store data on multiple hard drives. The operating system doesn't want to get into that complexity. The operating system basically just wants to say, hey, store this file for me, or hey, I want this file back. doesn't want to get into the complexity of somehow storing data across multiple disks. So that's where the RAID controller comes in. The RAID controller acts as an intermediary between the operating system and the actual hard drives. And now let's start with something important. In RAID 0, you require a minimum, minimum of two hard drives. You can have more, but in order to implement RAID 0, you need a minimum of two hard drives. And now let's look at how it works. So first thing that'll happen here is a file will come in from the operating system. The operating system is saying, hey, store this for me. Now in a RAID 0 array, what the operating system is going to do is what's called striping. Striping is where the RAID controller will split the file into two or more pieces. Since we have two hard drives here, and by the way, that's what disk 0 and disk 1 are here. These are our two hard drives. Since we have two hard drives here, the RAID controller is going to split the file into two equal sized pieces of data. And now the RAID controller can write those two chunks of data simultaneously in parallel to the two different hard drives. What's the benefit of this? Why are we doing this? Speed, performance, right? Because by the RAID controller striping, splitting the file into two pieces and then being able to write those two pieces simultaneously in parallel, we've effectively doubled our read write performance. Add another hard drive, you've tripled your read-write performance. Add another hard drive, you've quadrupled your read-write performance. So remember this. RAID 0 is all about speed, performance. This is so that you can read and write data a lot more quickly. There is, however, a massive downside. What would happen if we lost disk 0 here? And by lost, I mean if it failed and the data on this drive was unrecoverable. So if we lost disk zero, what would the consequence of that be? How much of our data would we lost? Well, technically, you would have lost half the data. But good luck opening a file that you have half of, right? So... Truly, if you lose either one of these hard drives, you've effectively lost all of your data. And this is something important to point out, because I know a lot of people, when they hear RAID, they think, oh, that makes your data more redundant. Not necessarily, right? A RAID 0 array actually makes it much more likely that you're going to lose all your data, right? Two hard drives, it's twice as likely you're going to lose all your data. Add another hard drive, it's three times as likely, right? So just remember this, RAID 0 is all about speed. It gives you a really nice performance boost, but it actually makes it much more likely that you're going to lose all of your data. So you're not going to want to use a RAID 0 array for some really critical data. So RAID 0, all about speed. And as more files come in, those files will be striped and written across these two or more hard drives. OK, so that's RAID 0. Let's now look at RAID 1 mirroring. And as you can probably guess by the name, you can figure out what the RAID controller is going to be doing here. By the way, RAID 1 mirroring requires same setup. You have a RAID controller and a minimum of two hard drives. Again, you can have more, but you require a minimum of two hard drives. so what happens in a raid one array well file comes in from the operating system and as the name implies mirroring what the raid controller is going to do is make two complete copies of the entire file it's going to mirror the file and then it's going to write these two entire copies simultaneously to two different hard drives What was the benefit of this? Well, it didn't really give us any speed improvement, right? Because we're still having to write the entire file to a hard drive. We happen to be doing it two at the same time, but we still have to write the entire file, so... Doesn't give us any speed performance writing, and it doesn't really give us any performance reading. So what's the benefit of this? And it's pretty obvious here, right? If we lose disk 0, no problem, right? We've still got another complete copy of the file here on disk 1. So RAID 1 is not about performance. It's about data redundancy. If you have some super critical data that you just cannot lose, you could have a RAID 1 array with 10 hard drives in it, and you could lose nine of those hard drives, and you'd still have your data. So you can go pretty crazy with this if you really want to, but RAID 1 mirroring is all about data redundancy, not about performance. Now, I kind of lied when I said that you only need to know about three different types of raid. There's actually another one that you need to know about. It's known as raid 10 or raid 1 plus 0. And it's exactly what it sounds like. Raid 10 or raid 1 plus 0 is just raid 1 and raid 0 combined. So RAID 10 requires a minimum of four hard drives. And by the way, the reason I keep emphasizing this is this is a really good exam question. What's the minimum number of hard drives required for RAID 10 or RAID 5 or RAID 1? So I'm going to highlight it here, and then I'll show you a nice summary table at the end. So RAID 10 requires a minimum of four hard drives. And the reason you need a minimum of four hard drives is because RAID 10 is both mirroring and striping. So what the RAID controller will do when a file comes in is the RAID controller will first mirror the file. So now we've got two complete copies of the file and the RAID controller will now stripe these files, split them in half because we've got four hard drives here. We'll split into four chunks. So now we've got four chunks of data that can be written to the four different hard drives simultaneously. Did this give us a performance boost? Yes, right? Because we were able to stripe the file, split it in half, and then write those two halves simultaneously, we've effectively doubled our read write performance. So that's awesome. We get a really nice speed improvement here. And we also have data redundancy. We can lose a hard drive. So if we lose disk 0 here, no big deal, right? We've got an exact copy of that data over here on disk 2. Or if we lose disk 1, no big deal, right? We've got an exact copy of that data over here on disk 3. So a RAID 10 array can suffer the loss of any one hard drive. Any one hard drive. Can it suffer the loss of any two hard drives? No, not any two. You could suffer. Like if it was, if it was, if you were lucky and you lost disc zero and disc one, you'd be fine, right? Because you still have the other half of the file over here on two and three. But what would happen if you lost zero and two? Well, you're toast, right? Because now you've lost both copies of this original chunk of the data. Or if you lost both disk one and disk three, you're toast. You've lost everything. So RAID 10 can only suffer the loss of any one hard drive. And I highlight this because at the very end, we're going to talk about a type of RAID that can suffer the loss of any two hard drives. We'll get there. So just remember, RAID 10 can only suffer the loss of any one hard drive. Okay, now let's talk about the third major type of RAID that you need to know about, which is known as RAID 5. And RAID 5 uses magical parity protection data. And I'll show you why it's magical in just a second here. So you can kind of think of RAID 5 as like the Goldilocks of RAID. It's like the happy medium. It gives you a nice performance boost. It gives you data redundancy. And it doesn't require as many hard drives. That's the big downside with RAID 10. It requires a lot of hard drives, minimum of four. But you can have way more than that in a RAID 10 array. RAID 5 is like the happy medium. It gives you speed, gives you performance, and it doesn't require as many hard drives. You require a minimum of three. So here's how a RAID 5 controller works. Here's what a RAID 5 controller does with the data. So the first thing the RAID controller does is striping. It splits the file in half. But now, here's where the magical parity protection data comes in. The next thing that the RAID controller does is it calculates what's known as some parity data. And the mathematical function that it uses here is known as the exclusive OR function. Oh, and my light board isn't turned on, so you can't see it at all. There we go. Exclusive OR. So the mathematical function that is being used here by the RAID controller to calculate this magical parity data is known as the exclusive OR function. And this parity data is going to be the same size as one of the chunks. So let's say if we take a step back here. If this was a two megabyte file, when we stripe the file, split it in half, we're going to have a one megabyte chunk here. We're going to have another one megabyte chunk here. And the magical parity protection data is going to also be one megabyte. It's going to be the same size as one half of it, like one of the chunks. So what we end up with here is three chunks of data that are all equally sized. Three equal sized chunks of data that we can then write simultaneously to three different hard drives. So first of all, do we get a performance boost? Yes, right? Because we've striped, right? The RAID controller has striped the file. We're able to write this file, these two halves of the file simultaneously to do two different hard drives. Now, it's not quite as fast as, say, like simple RAID 0, because the RAID controller did have to take some time to calculate this parity data. So that does slow things down a little bit, but it's still faster than a single hard drive by itself. So we get some nice performance boost from this. And now, how do we get data redundancy? Well, if we lose the parity data here, who cares, right? We've still got the two original chunks of data. So we obviously don't care about losing the parity data. But here's where the magical parity data protection comes in. This is really cool. So what's going to happen if we lose disk 0 here, right? That's bad. This is one of the original chunks of data. Using the remaining original chunk of data here on disk one and the magical parity protection data here on disk two, we can actually reconstruct, recalculate the original lost chunk that would have been on disk zero. So if this hard drive fails, we replace it with a new empty hard drive. And then this takes a while, but the RAID controller can actually use the remaining original chunk of data on disk one here and the magical parody production data to reconstruct this lost chunk of data. Super cool. And it doesn't just work for rate disk 0. This is why the parity protection data is so magical. What would happen if we lost disk 1 here? Well, we can recover from it. We still have one remaining chunk of original data here on disk 0. And using the magical parity protection data, we can reconstruct the lost chunk on disk 1. So in other words, you can lose either one of the original chunks of data and using the other remaining original chunk and the magical parody production data, you can recalculate the lost chunk of data. So that's why I like to refer to it as magical parody production data, because it's kind of cool. You can lose either one of the original chunks and you can recalculate that lost chunk of data using the remaining original chunk and the magical parity protection data. And this is why RAID 5 gives you redundancy, because you can lose any one of these hard drives and you can still recover. And by the way, this is beyond the scope of the exam, but typically you don't have just one hard drive dedicated to all of your RAID data. It's typically sort of a round robin function, but it varies by RAID type and implementation. But that's RAID 5. And here's where I definitely lie even more about just needing to know three types of RAID. There's actually one more type of RAID that you need to know about, but you actually know exactly how it works. It's known as RAID 6. What RAID 6 is, is basically RAID 5, except instead of calculating one chunk of pair D data, the RAID controller actually calculates two separate chunks of pair D data. And so what RAID 6 requires is a minimum, minimum of four hard drives. Because in a RAID 6 array, you're going to have the original, one half of the original file, the other half of the original file, and then parity chunk, parity chunk. So RAID 6 requires a minimum of four hard drives. But here's the really, really cool thing that it provides. It provides double parity protection, which means... And here's the really key thing to remember for the exam. RAID 6 can handle loss of any two hard drives. You can lose, for instance, both original chunks of data. And you can actually recalculate those two original chunks using the two chunks of parity data. Or you could lose both chunks of parity data. Or you could lose one original and one parity data. You can recover from all these situations. So the critical thing you need to remember about RAID 6... is that it provides double parity protection, which means that a RAID 6 array can suffer the loss of any two original hard drives. Any two. Doesn't matter which ones. RAID 6 can suffer the loss of any two hard drives. Here's a handy dandy little summary here. So key things to remember, RAID 0 does not provide any data redundancy at all. In fact, RAID 0 makes it much more likely that you're gonna lose all of your data. And the critical thing to remember about RAID 6 is it provides double parity. So RAID 6 can suffer the loss of any two hard drives. And then as you can see here, with these giant numbers, we're really highlighting the minimum number of hard drives that you need here. So RAID 0 and RAID 1 each require a minimum of two hard drives. RAID 10 and RAID 6 require a minimum of four hard drives, and RAID 5 requires a minimum of three. So remember those minimum drive numbers. Okay, one more little concept to cover here. This actually doesn't have anything specifically to do with RAID. So CRC, cyclical redundancy checks. This is a way of really quickly checking for accidental changes to data typically implemented at a hardware level. So the reason we're kind of talking about this here is we've just talked about hard drives. They're part of a rate array. CRC checks are often used in hard drives. What will happen is that as a hard drive is writing data to the platter, the hard drive will basically read it back to itself really quickly to make sure that it didn't make any writing errors. Or when it's reading data, it'll basically read the data twice to make sure it didn't make any read errors. So you can use CRC checks on hard drives to quickly check for accidental changes to data. But it's not just on hard drives. CRC checks are often used in RAM. So when you're reading, when a system is reading or writing data to RAM. CRC checks can be done really quickly. Typically, again, implemented at a hardware level to check for accidental changes to data. And CRC checks are even often used in network communications. So when a system is turning ones and zeros into plus or minus five voltages on a copper wire, CRC checks can be used to see if there's any, again, any accidental changes to data when it's being written across the network. Just remember this, CRC's cyclical redundancy checks are a way of really quickly checking for accidental changes to data, a bit flip or something like that.
